message.content:
Adversaries may achieve persistence by creating or modifying shortcuts that execute programs during system boot or user login. These shortcuts, often placed in the startup folder, can be used to launch malicious tools. Common exploitation methods include creating new shortcuts, modifying existing ones, or using masquerading techniques to make malicious shortcuts appear legitimate. Notable examples include APT39, Astaroth, and Lazarus Group, which have used .LNK files to maintain persistence. The impact of this technique is significant as it allows malware to persist through reboots and user logins, potentially leading to prolonged unauthorized access and control.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting changes to startup folders and shortcut files, employing application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized programs from executing, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to detect and respond to suspicious shortcut modifications. Regular audits of startup items and user education on the risks of malicious shortcuts can also help mitigate this threat.
